perturbation.original_text: Section 2.4 Document Disposition . The Confidential Information (except for Derivative Materials), and all copies thereof, will remain the absolute property of the Company and will be returned to the Company or destroyed (and such destruction confirmed in writing by an officer of the Recipient) without retaining any copies thereof immediately upon the Company’s request. Derivative Materials will be immediately destroyed at the request of the Company and such destruction will be confirmed to the Company in writing by an officer of the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 2.4 Document Disposition . The Confidential Information (except for Derivative Materials), and all copies thereof, will remain the absolute property of the Company and will be returned to the Company or destroyed (and such destruction confirmed in writing by an officer of the Recipient) without retaining any copies thereof within 30 days upon the Company’s request. Derivative Materials will be immediately destroyed at the request of the Company and such destruction will be confirmed to the Company in writing by an officer of the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: The modification to Section 2.4, introducing a 30-day timeframe for the return or destruction of Confidential Information, conflicts with the original requirement of immediate action. This creates an in-text contradiction because the original wording implies that action should be taken without delay, while the updated text allows for a 30-day delay, causing confusion about the expected timeline for compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 2.4 Document Disposition . The Confidential Information (except for Derivative Materials), and all copies thereof, will remain the absolute property of the Company and will be returned to the Company or destroyed (and such destruction confirmed in writing by an officer of the Recipient) without retaining any copies thereof within 30 days upon the Company’s request. Derivative Materials will be immediately destroyed at the request of the Company and such destruction will be confirmed to the Company in writing by an officer of the Recipient.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires immediate return or destruction of confidential information. The changed text introduces a 30-day window for this action. The contradicted text states the 30-day requirement, but the original text doesn't, creating an inconsistency regarding the timeline for compliance, which constitutes an in-text contradiction as defined by 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' where different sections provide conflicting deadlines.

perturbation.original_text: Section 5.4 Remedies . The Recipient agrees that due to the nature of this Agreement and the Confidential Information, money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient and that the Company shall be entitled to seek specific performance, injunctive and/or other equitable relief as a remedy for any such breach. Such remedy shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedy of the Company for any breach by the Recipient of this Agreement, but shall be in addition to all other remedies available to the Company at law or in equity. The Recipient hereby waives any requirement for the securing or posting of any bond in connection with such remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 5.4 Remedies . The Recipient agrees that due to the nature of this Agreement and the Confidential Information, money damages would be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient. Such remedy shall be deemed to be the exclusive remedy of the Company for any breach by the Recipient of this Agreement and exclude all other remedies available to the Company at law or in equity. The Recipient hereby waives any requirement for the securing or posting of any bond in connection with such remedy.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that money damages would be a sufficient remedy and that it should be deemed the exclusive remedy, the modified Section 5.4 directly contradicts the initial text which stated that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy and that it would be added to all other remedies, creating uncertainty about the remedies available to the Company in the event of a breach.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 5.4 Remedies . The Recipient agrees that due to the nature of this Agreement and the Confidential Information, money damages would be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient. Such remedy shall be deemed to be the exclusive remedy of the Company for any breach by the Recipient of this Agreement and exclude all other remedies available to the Company at law or in equity. The Recipient hereby waives any requirement for the securing or posting of any bond in connection with such remedy.
perturbation.justification: The modified Section 5.4 explicitly states that money damages would be a sufficient and exclusive remedy, directly contradicting the original text's assertion that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy and that the Company shall be entitled to seek specific performance, injunctive and/or other equitable relief as a remedy, which is also what the contradicted text states. This inconsistency in remedies creates a strong contradiction, impacting the enforceability and understanding of the contract.

perturbation.original_text: Section 1.1 Certain Definitions . In addition to the terms defined elsewhere in this Agreement, the following terms used in this Agreement shall be construed to have the meanings set forth or referenced below: The term “ Confidential Information ” refers to (i) all business or financial information (including business plans, budgets, forecasts and financial projections) of the Company or its subsidiaries (or of others having business relationships with the Company), or concerning the Matter, whether or not marked or otherwise identified as confidential or proprietary, (ii) any other information or materials marked or designated as “confidential” or “proprietary” by the Company at the time of its disclosure to the Recipient and (iii) any other information or materials that the Company maintains as confidential or that is proprietary to the Company, the nature of which or the circumstances surrounding the disclosure of which would indicate to a reasonable person that such information or materials are confidential or proprietary. The foregoing information and materials shall be “ Confidential Information ” whether evidenced, transferred or transmitted in writing, orally, visually, electronically or by any other means, and whether disclosed before or after the date of this Agreement. “ Confidential Information ” shall also include all Derivative Materials. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary in this Agreement, “ Confidential Information ” shall not include information that (A) is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of a breach of this Agreement by the Recipient; or (B) is or becomes available to the Recipient on a non-confidential basis from any source other than the Company or its Representatives, which source has represented to the Recipient (and that the Recipient reasonably believes after due inquiry) that such source is entitled to disclose such information without the Recipient being bound by any obligation of confidentiality, provided that upon the Recipient becoming aware that such information is Confidential Information that the source was not entitled to disclose, this Agreement shall thereafter apply to such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 1.1 Certain Definitions . In addition to the terms defined elsewhere in this Agreement, the following terms used in this Agreement shall be construed to have the meanings set forth or referenced below: The term “ Confidential Information ” refers to (i) all business or financial information (including business plans, budgets, forecasts and financial projections) of the Company or its subsidiaries (or of others having business relationships with the Company), or concerning the Matter, whether or not marked or otherwise identified as confidential or proprietary, (ii) any other information or materials marked or designated as “confidential” or “proprietary” by the Recipient at the time of its disclosure to the Company and (iii) any other information or materials that the Recipient maintains as confidential or that is proprietary to the Recipient, the nature of which or the circumstances surrounding the disclosure of which would indicate to a reasonable person that such information or materials are confidential or proprietary. The foregoing information and materials shall be “ Confidential Information ” whether evidenced, transferred or transmitted in writing, orally, visually, electronically or by any other means, and whether disclosed before or after the date of this Agreement. “ Confidential Information ” shall also include all Derivative Materials. Notwithstanding anything to the contrary in this Agreement, “ Confidential Information ” shall not include information that (A) is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of a breach of this Agreement by the Company; or (B) is or becomes available to the Recipient on a non-confidential basis from any source other than the Company or its Representatives, which source has represented to the Recipient (and that the Recipient reasonably believes after due inquiry) that such source is entitled to disclose such information without the Recipient being bound by any obligation of confidentiality, provided that upon the Recipient becoming aware that such information is Confidential Information that the source was not entitled to disclose, this Agreement shall thereafter apply to such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The perturbation changes the definition of 'Confidential Information' in Section 1.1 to include information belonging to the Recipient, not just the Company. This contradicts the core purpose of the agreement, which is to protect the Company's Confidential Information. This alteration creates uncertainty because various sections, such as Section 2.1 (Non-Disclosure and Non-Use Covenants), now become ambiguous. Is the Recipient obligated to protect its own information from itself? This creates an in-text contradiction regarding the agreement's scope and purpose.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 2.1 Non-Disclosure and Non-Use Covenants . As a condition to the Company’s disclosure of the Confidential Information to the Recipient, the Recipient hereby covenants and agrees that all Confidential Information will be kept confidential by the Recipient and will not, without the prior written consent of the Company, be disclosed by the Recipient, in any manner whatsoever, in whole or in part, and will not be used by the Recipient, directly or indirectly, for any purpose other than facilitating discussion with the Company. Moreover, the Recipient agrees to transmit the Confidential Information to only those Representatives who need to know the Confidential Information for the purpose of facilitating the Recipient’s discussion with the Company and who are informed of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information and the terms of this Agreement. The Recipient hereby acknowledges that it is aware, and the Recipient agrees that it will advise its Representatives who are informed as to the matters which are the subject of this Agreement, that (i) the Confidential Information being furnished may contain or may itself be material, non-public information concerning the Company, and (ii) securities laws in the United States prohibit any person who has received material, non-public information concerning the Company or the matters which are the subject of this Agreement from purchasing or selling securities of the Company or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities. The Recipient agrees to not engage, directly or indirectly, in transactions in Company securities while in possession of Confidential 2 Information, and further agrees that is will be responsible for its compliance with federal and state securities laws. The Recipient will be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by the Recipient or by its Representatives.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include the Recipient's own information, which directly contradicts Section 2.1, which states that the Recipient's obligations pertain to the Company's Confidential Information. The original text defines Confidential Information as related to the Company, but the changed text includes Recipient information, creating a conflict in Section 2.1 because the Recipient can't be obligated to keep their own information confidential from themselves according to Section 2.1, which says 'As a condition to the Company’s disclosure of the Confidential Information to the Recipient, the Recipient hereby covenants and agrees that all Confidential Information will be kept confidential by the Recipient.' The change creates an inconsistency as defined by the Perturbation Type: Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: Section 5.8 Miscellaneous . This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement of the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and it replaces and supersedes all prior agreements between the Parties. No provision of this Agreement may be waived or amended unless such waiver or amendment is in writing. No failure or delay by the Company in exercising any right, power or privilege under this Agreement shall operate as a waiver thereof nor shall any single or partial exercise thereof preclude any other or further exercise of any right, power or privilege hereunder. This Agreement may not be assigned by the Recipient, by operation of law or otherwise, without the Company’s prior written consent.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 5.8 Miscellaneous . This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement of the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and it replaces and supersedes all prior agreements between the Parties. No provision of this Agreement may be waived or amended unless such waiver or amendment is in writing or orally agreed upon by both parties. No failure or delay by the Company in exercising any right, power or privilege under this Agreement shall operate as a waiver thereof nor shall any single or partial exercise thereof preclude any other or further exercise of any right, power or privilege hereunder. This Agreement may not be assigned by the Recipient, by operation of law or otherwise, without the Company’s prior written consent.
perturbation.explanation: By altering Section 5.8 to allow for oral amendments, the text now creates an in-text contradiction with the previous statement mandating that all waivers or amendments must be in writing. This change creates uncertainty about how the agreement can be legally modified and if the amendment should be in writing or oral.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 5.8 Miscellaneous . This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement of the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and it replaces and supersedes all prior agreements between the Parties. No provision of this Agreement may be waived or amended unless such waiver or amendment is in writing or orally agreed upon by both parties. No failure or delay by the Company in exercising any right, power or privilege under this Agreement shall operate as a waiver thereof nor shall any single or partial exercise thereof preclude any other or further exercise of any right, power or privilege hereunder. This Agreement may not be assigned by the Recipient, by operation of law or otherwise, without the Company’s prior written consent.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 5.8 introduces an in-text contradiction. The original text stated that amendments must be in writing, but the changed text allows for oral amendments as well. The Contradicted Text also states that amendments must be in writing or orally agreed upon by both parties. This creates a direct contradiction within the same section because it becomes unclear whether amendments must be in writing or can be oral, leading to potential disputes about the validity of modifications to the agreement. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition.
